1: YHWH tells Aaron: 'You and your sons and your father's house with you shall bear iniquity connected with the sanctuary.' The Aaronic priesthood bears the iniquity — the ritual risk and responsibility — of the sanctuary. The priest stands in the gap not only with his censer (as Aaron did in Numbers 16:47–48) but with his entire accountability: he bears what others cannot bear. This is the priestly substitution principle that finds its ultimate form in Christ, who 'himself bore our sins in his body on the tree' (1 Peter 2:24) — not sharing the risk but absorbing the full iniquity.
2: The Levites are associated with Aaron and his sons and are to be joined to Aaron and serve him. The Levites serve the priests, not the other way around — a subordination within the sacred order. In the NT, the serving principle is extended and inverted by Christ: 'whoever would be first among you must be your slave, even as the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many' (Matthew 20:27–28). The servant-hierarchy of the Levitical order is subverted and fulfilled by Christ who descends to the lowest service.
3: The Levites serve the priests and the whole congregation, but they must not come near the furnishings or the altar lest both they and the priests die. The graduated holiness zones are maintained: Levites support but do not touch the most holy things. The death-warning for unauthorized touching mirrors the Adam-and-Eve prohibition: access beyond what is given leads to death. Christ removes the prohibition not by lowering the standard of holiness but by providing a new basis for access through his blood (Hebrews 10:19).
4: The Levites are to join with the priests and keep charge of the tent of meeting for all the service of the tent. The priestly system requires the Levitical support system — the complex of the temple/tabernacle cannot function without both orders working together. The NT church likewise requires both those called to specific leadership and the whole body in its supporting roles: 'the whole body, joined and held together by every joint with which it is equipped... makes the body grow so that it builds itself up in love' (Ephesians 4:16).
5: Aaron and his sons are to keep charge of the sanctuary and the altar, so that wrath may come on the people of Israel no more. The priestly charge is protective: the priests' faithful service guards the congregation from covenant-wrath. Christ's perpetual high-priestly intercession is the ultimate form of this protective function: 'he always lives to make intercession for them' (Hebrews 7:25). The priests' guarding of the altar between the people and the divine holiness is the OT type of Christ's mediatorial standing.
6: YHWH has taken the Levites from among Israel — they are given as a gift (netunah, given ones) to Aaron and his sons, dedicated to YHWH. The language of being 'given' as a divine gift pervades the Levitical appointment theology: the Levites are YHWH's gift to Aaron, and they are YHWH's possession. In the NT, Christ says to the Father: 'I have manifested your name to the people whom you gave me out of the world' (John 17:6) — the disciples are the Father's gift to the Son, the NT form of the Levitical gift.
7: Aaron and his sons are to attend to their priesthood for everything concerning the altar and behind the veil. Any outsider who approaches shall be put to death. The death-penalty for unauthorized access to the most holy things is the enforcement clause of the holiness gradient. Hebrews 9:6–8 explains that the restricted access of the outer court and holy place, and the once-a-year access of the high priest alone to the holy of holies, was the Spirit's signaling that 'the way into the holy places is not yet opened as long as the first section is still standing.' The death-warning for unauthorized access was the truth-in-type that Christ's death would fulfill: he died in the place of those who would enter, so that they could enter through him.
8: YHWH speaks to Aaron about the priestly portion — the 'charge of my contributions' (the raised offerings) are given to the priests as their share. The priestly income is not extracted from the people but is the divinely designated share of the offerings — YHWH gives the priests their portion. The principle that those who serve the altar share in the altar's offerings is cited by Paul in 1 Corinthians 9:13–14 as the OT basis for the NT principle: 'the Lord commanded that those who proclaim the gospel should get their living by the gospel.'
9: From the most holy offerings — grain offering, sin offering, guilt offering — the priests receive a portion: everything not burned is theirs as most holy, for them and their sons. The most holy things are the priests' food. The priest eating the sin offering (Leviticus 6:26) is one of the OT's most remarkable theological acts: the sin-bearer eats the sin offering, absorbing the offering that absorbed the sin. Christ is simultaneously the high priest who offers and the sacrifice that is offered (Hebrews 9:14: 'who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God').
10: The most holy things are eaten in the most holy place — only by males, as most holy. The gradated access (most holy place, males only, only the priests' sons) mirrors the graduated holiness of the camp architecture: the closer to the holy of holies, the more restricted the access. Christ abolishes the male/female distinction in access (Galatians 3:28), and tears the veil (Matthew 27:51) that restricted the holy of holies — but he does not abolish the principle that access comes through him, the appointed High Priest.
11: The raised offerings of the congregation — the wave offerings — are given to the priests and their sons and daughters as their portion. The daughters of the priests may eat of the raised offerings (compare Numbers 18:13). The daughters' inclusion in the priestly food portion (though not in the most holy things of Numbers 18:10) creates a graduated access within the priestly household itself: full access for priestly males, partial access for the household. The household-of-God concept (those who live in the priest's house are fed by his service) anticipates the church as household: 'those who are of the household of God' (Ephesians 2:19) are fed by Christ's priestly sacrifice.
12: The best of the oil, the best of the wine, the best of the grain — the firstfruits given to YHWH are the priests' portion. The principle that YHWH's portion is the 'first' and the 'best' runs throughout the OT (Exodus 23:19; Proverbs 3:9: 'Honor YHWH with your wealth and with the firstfruits of all your produce'). Christ is 'the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep' (1 Corinthians 15:20) — both the first who rose and YHWH's best offering to humanity, and humanity's best offering to YHWH (2 Corinthians 5:21: 'made him to be sin who knew no sin').
13: The firstfruits of all that is in their land (which they bring to YHWH) shall be the priests' — everyone who is clean in the priestly house may eat them. The firstfruit offerings are the priests' food because the priests represent the God to whom the firstfruits belong. The NT firstfruit concept: 'You yourselves like living stones are being built up as a spiritual house, to be a holy priesthood, to offer spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God through Jesus Christ' (1 Peter 2:5) — the whole church is the priestly house that receives and offers the firstfruits of the new creation.
14: Every devoted thing in Israel shall be theirs. The ḥerem (devoted/consecrated thing) belongs entirely to YHWH and therefore to YHWH's priests. What is utterly consecrated to God — beyond ordinary use — flows through the priestly order. Christ as the one 'set apart' (ḥerem-principle applied to the holy) is given by the Father and given back through the Son to all who receive him (John 3:16; Romans 8:32: 'he who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all').
15: The firstborn of every womb, human or animal, that is offered to YHWH, belongs to the priests — except human firstborns and unclean animal firstborns, which must be redeemed. The firstborn redemption principle (Exodus 13:2,12–15; Numbers 3:47–48) recurs: every human firstborn must be redeemed through a payment, not sacrificed. Christ, the firstborn of all creation (Colossians 1:15), is not redeemed but is himself the redemption price for all firstborn humanity.
16: The human firstborn is redeemed from the age of one month for five shekels — the holy shekel, which is twenty gerahs. The specific five-shekel redemption price connects to Luke 2:22–24: Mary and Joseph 'brought him up to Jerusalem to present him to the Lord (as it is written in the Law of the Lord, “Every male who first opens the womb shall be called holy to the Lord”) and to offer a sacrifice.' Jesus' parents paid this exact Levitical firstborn fee for the Son who was himself the ultimate firstborn redeemer.
17: The firstborn of an ox, sheep, or goat — their blood is sprinkled on the altar and their fat is burned — these are not redeemed; they are sacrificed. The distinction between redeemable (humans, unclean animals) and unredeemable (clean animals — they are sacrificed) creates the sacrifice/redemption theology: some things are redeemed by payment; others are taken by death. The clean animal firstborn that cannot be redeemed-by-payment but must be offered by death is the type of Christ — he was not redeemed by payment but offered by death (1 Peter 1:18–19: 'not with perishable things such as silver or gold... but with the precious blood of Christ').
18: The flesh of the sacrificed firstborns — like the breast of the wave offering and the right thigh — goes to the priests. The priestly food-cycle is closed: the sacrificed firstborns' flesh feeds the priests who offer them. The priest is sustained by the very sacrifice he performs. This reflects the NT principle that ministers of the gospel are sustained by the gospel they proclaim (1 Corinthians 9:14) — they eat, as it were, from the altar at which they serve.
19: All the holy contributions of the Israelites — the wave offerings — are given to the priests as a perpetual due, a covenant of salt forever before YHWH. The 'covenant of salt' (brit melach) is an idiom for an inviolable, enduring covenant — salt was used to preserve and to covenant (Leviticus 2:13; Ezra 4:14). The priestly income covenant is as permanent and incorruptible as salt. Christ's own covenant with his people is similarly described by Hebrews as guaranteed by an oath (Hebrews 6:17–18) and by his own blood — a covenant that cannot be dissolved.
20: YHWH tells Aaron: 'You shall have no inheritance in their land, neither shall you have any portion among them. I am your portion and your inheritance among the people of Israel.' The priest's inheritance is YHWH himself — not land, not goods, not tribal allotment, but the divine person. Psalms 16:5–6 (applied to Christ in Acts 2:25–31) echoes this: 'The LORD is my chosen portion and my cup; you hold my lot. The lines have fallen for me in pleasant places; indeed, I have a beautiful inheritance.' Christ, the true high priest, has as his inheritance the Father himself — and shares this inheritance with those in him (John 17:22–23: 'The glory that you have given me I have given to them').
21: The Levites receive every tithe in Israel as their inheritance in exchange for their service in the tent of meeting. The Levitical tithe is their substitute for land-inheritance: instead of 12 equal tribal allotments, they receive the tithe-income from the whole people. This is an early form of institutional funding: those who serve the people's worship are financially supported by the people's giving. Hebrews 7:5 uses this verse in the Melchizedek argument — the Levites 'who receive the priestly office have a commandment... to take tithes from the people' — to show that the Melchizedekian priesthood (Christ's) is greater, since even Levi paid tithes through Abraham to Melchizedek.
22: The Israelites must not come near the tent of meeting, lest they bear sin and die. The Levitical protective function (bearing the risk, so the congregation doesn't) continues. The Levites serve as a human buffer between the congregation and the lethal holiness of the sanctuary. Christ removes this buffer not by making holiness less lethal but by absorbing its lethal force himself: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' (Galatians 3:13).
23: The Levites do the service of the tent of meeting and bear their iniquity. 'Bearing iniquity' (yisˀu ˁavonam) is the language of priestly substitution — the Levites carry the guilt-risk of the congregation's proximity to the sanctuary. The same phrase applies to the suffering servant: 'he shall bear their iniquities' (Isaiah 53:11). The Levitical bearing of iniquity is the national-scale type; the servant's individual bearing is the eschatological fulfillment.
24: The Levites receive the tithe as their inheritance because they have no land-inheritance. The two-tier system (land for the twelve tribes, tithe for Levi) is the economic structure of covenant Israel. The Levites' landlessness is their distinctive mark: they are the tribe whose inheritance is YHWH-and-people, not earth-and-boundary. The church, as a 'priesthood,' likewise holds its inheritance in Christ rather than in earthly land — 'an inheritance that is imperishable, undefiled, and unfading, kept in heaven' (1 Peter 1:4).
25: YHWH instructs Moses to speak to the Levites about their obligation: when they receive the tithe, they must offer a tithe of the tithe to YHWH as a raised offering. The Levites tithe on what they receive — they are not exempt from giving just because they receive rather than hold land. The principle: everyone within the covenant community tithes to YHWH; no one stands outside the giving relationship. 2 Corinthians 9:7 applies this to the church: 'Each one must give as he has decided in his heart, not reluctantly or under compulsion, for God loves a cheerful giver.'
26: The Levites give a tithe of the tithe — the best of everything — to Aaron the priest. The tithe-of-the-tithe goes to the chief priest. The graduated offering system creates a flow: the people give a tithe to the Levites; the Levites give a tithe of that to the high priest. The high priest receives the best of what flows through the entire giving chain. Christ, as the ultimate High Priest, receives the firstfruits and best of all that is offered — 'that in everything he might be preeminent' (Colossians 1:18).
27: The tithe given by the Levites shall be credited to them as if it were grain from the threshing floor or wine from the winepress. YHWH treats the Levites' tithe-of-tithe as equivalent to the agricultural firstfruits of the land-holding tribes. The principle of spiritual equivalence: the Levite who gives from what he receives is counted by YHWH as equivalent to the farmer who gives from what he grows. YHWH accepts the offering according to the giver's ability and faithfulness, not according to the material source (compare Mark 12:41–44: the widow's two small coins received as more than the large gifts of the wealthy).
28: The Levites' raised offering is given to Aaron the priest. The practical mechanics of the tithe-flow complete the institutional structure: tribe gives to Levite; Levite gives best to Aaron; Aaron oversees the most holy things. The entire system is a material embodiment of the theological structure: the many serve the covenant purpose; the few most-dedicated bear the most holy responsibilities; the appointed high priest stands at the center of it all. Christ stands at the center of a new covenant equivalent: all spiritual service flows toward and through the one who has presented himself as the ultimate offering.
29: The best of all that is given to the Levites must be given as YHWH's raised offering — the best part, the holy portion. 'The best of it' (mishmenno et-mikdesho — from its fatness, its holy part) is what must be offered. The principle that YHWH receives the best — not the surplus, not the second-rate — runs from Abel's firstlings (Genesis 4:4) through the priestly system to the NT appeal: 'present your bodies as a living sacrifice, holy and acceptable to God' (Romans 12:1). Christ himself is the Father's best offering to the world (John 3:16).
30: When the Levites offer the best of the tithe, the remainder may be counted as their produce from threshing floor or winepress. Having given the best, the remainder is theirs freely. The principle: first give the best to YHWH; then enjoy the rest without guilt. Proverbs 3:9–10: 'Honor YHWH with your wealth and with the firstfruits of all your produce; then your barns will be filled with plenty.' The agricultural and priestly firstfruit principle is a material enactment of the deeper truth: those who give Christ first priority in life will find that what remains is richly provided for (Matthew 6:33).
31: The Levites may eat the tithe-remainder in any place — it is their wages for their service in the tent of meeting. The word 'wages' (sakar) for Levitical service is significant: they are workers deserving pay, not charity cases. 1 Timothy 5:18 applies this directly: 'The laborer deserves his wages' — citing both Deuteronomy 25:4 and this Levitical principle as the basis for financially supporting those who minister the word.
32: 'You shall bear no sin by reason of it, when you have contributed the best of it.' The Levites are cleared of guilt when they give YHWH's portion first. The guilt-avoidance principle is the reverse of the authorization-principle: just as unauthorized access brings death (Numbers 16:40; Numbers 18:7), authorized giving of the best releases the giver from guilt. The clearance from sin through faithful giving is a shadow of the fuller clearance from sin through Christ's atonement — 'there is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus' (Romans 8:1). The sacred things of YHWH must not be profaned: this final warning closes the chapter by returning to the holiness-principle that underlies all of Num 16–18.
